--- SOURCE 1: An overview on modelling approaches for photochemical ... ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124

SUMMARY:
Photo-electrochemical and photocatalytic technologies are promising solutions for solar fuel production and involve a number of physical and chemical phenomena. c r e d,b u l k Surface concentration of species involved in the cathodic reaction This review emphasizes AI’s transformative role in enhancing biomass bioenergy production, positioning it as a critical tool for sustainable energy solutions and future environmental policies to achieve carbon neutrality. Energy, exergy, and enviro-economic analysis was conducted on the PV/T system with each HTFs. The results show that the thermal efficiency of PV/T was enhanced by 5.39, 3.87, and 9.45 % with C-dot, MXene and hybrid NFs, respectively. Solar energy is efficiently converted into electron-hole pairs, which play a pivotal role in water-splitting reactions occurring at the electrode surfaces.

FULL CONTENT:
An overview on modelling approaches for photochemical and photoelectrochemical solar fuels processes and technologies - ScienceDirect

Review

An overview on modelling approaches for photochemical and photoelectrochemical solar fuels processes and technologies
=============================================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Gabriele Falciani, Eliodoro Chiavazzo

Show more

Outline

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.enconman.2023.117366](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.enconman.2023.117366 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S0196890423007124&orderBeanReset=true)

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/)

Open access

Highlights
----------

*   •Continuum and kinetic models for the description of photocatalytic processes. 
*   •Effect of the nanoscale phenomena on the photocatalytic performance at the macroscale. 
*   •Coupling coarse-grained and continuum models to investigate the reactive interfaces. 

Abstract
--------

Photo-electrochemical and [photocatalytic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/photocatalytic "Learn more about photocatalytic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") technologies are promising solutions for solar fuel production and involve a number of physical and chemical phenomena. We provide an overview of numerical and analytical tools to describe such phenomena occurring at disparate time and space scales within devices such as photo-electrochemical cells and photo-chemical reactors. On one hand, chemical phenomena include photo-induced [electron transfer](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/electron-transfer "Learn more about electron transfer from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), charge separation, recombination, [equilibrium reactions](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/equilibrium-reaction "Learn more about equilibrium reactions from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") between species in solutions and adsorption reactions. On the other hand, examples of [physical phenomena](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/physical-phenomena "Learn more about physical phenomena from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are the transport of [chemical species](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/chemical-specie "Learn more about chemical species from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") or self-assembling of molecular structures. In this respect, we critically review [macroscale](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/macroscale "Learn more about macroscale from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[continuum models](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/continuum-model "Learn more about continuum models from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") for transport phenomena combined with kinetic descriptions including their possible coupling with models at even lower scales. We specifically focus on atomistic and coarse-grained models able to represent the local environment of the reactive interfaces such as [photoelectrodes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/photoelectrode "Learn more about photoelectrodes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") or supra-molecular assemblies. The critical role of the latter structures on photochemical conversion is highlighted: Therefore, morphological structure of self-assemblies, such as [micelles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/micelle "Learn more about micelles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and monolayers, in solution and at the solid–liquid or gas–liquid interfaces are also discussed. Finally, important scientific gaps are identified and possible perspectives for future research outlooked.

1. Solar fuel production processes
----------------------------------

Global population growth and rising energy demand pose significant challenges to a sustainable future [[1]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b1). The use of [fossil fuels](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/fossil-fuel "Learn more about fossil fuels from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and the emission of greenhouse gases, particularly [carbon dioxide](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/carbon-dioxide "Learn more about carbon dioxide from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), into the atmosphere are major contributors to [climate change](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/climate-change "Learn more about climate change from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[[2]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b2). The world energy demand is expected to double by 2050 and triple by the end of the twenty-first century [[3]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b3). Both policymakers and the general public are concerned about the depletion of fossil fuels, the environmental consequences of their use and importantly possible mitigations actions [[4]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b4). Hence, a significant shift in the global energy consumption mix is needed in the upcoming years, inducing scientists and engineers to search for zero-carbon, [renewable energy sources](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/renewable-energy-source "Learn more about renewable energy sources from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") such as wind, solar, hydropower, and geothermal to fulfill this energy demand [[5]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b5), [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b6). [Synthetic fuels](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/synthetic-fuel "Learn more about Synthetic fuels from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") generated from renewable sources can be one of the possible solutions to decrease the impact of our society on the environment [[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b7), [[8]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b8). An obvious choice to produce synthetic fuels is to exploit solar energy, which has an enormous potential as a clean, abundant, and economical energy source [[9]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b9), [[10]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b10), [[11]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b11). The generated solar fuel would be an energy vector able to overcome the main problem related to solar energy, namely its intermittency [[12]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b12). Hydrogen is one of the possible solar fuels and can be obtained from water splitting reducing protons while oxidizing water [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b13), [[14]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b14), [[15]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b15). It has the highest [energy density](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/energy-density "Learn more about energy density from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") among the fuels (120 MJ/kg) and when burnt generates H 2 O as byproduct. Moreover, it can be used in existing [internal combustion engines](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/internal-combustion-engine "Learn more about internal combustion engines from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), fuel cells and [gas turbines](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/gas-turbine "Learn more about gas turbines from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[[16]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b16). However, being the lightest element, both its storage and distribution are challenging [[17]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b17). As a complementary approach, researchers have focused their efforts in using CO 2 as a potential resource to be converted in [carbon based fuels](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/carbon-based-fuel "Learn more about carbon based fuels from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), from which also carbon based chemicals can be obtained [[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b7), [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b18). Indeed, carbon, capture, storage and utilization technologies are receiving an increasing attention [[19]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b19).

CO 2 is a thermodynamically stable and chemically inert molecule. This makes the reduction of CO 2 a challenging process due to both thermodynamics and kinetics [[20]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b20). Multiple electron (and proton) transfers are required in order to reduce CO 2 to valuable products through several radical intermediates [[21]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b21). A possible first step in the [carbon dioxide reduction](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/carbon-dioxide-reduction "Learn more about carbon dioxide reduction from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") process is the addition of a single electron to the CO 2 molecule which causes a bending of the linear molecular structure owing to the repulsion of the acquired electron located on the carbon atom and the [free electron](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/free-electron "Learn more about free electron from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") pairs on the two oxygen atoms. This configuration makes the [anion radical](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/radical-anion "Learn more about anion radical from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") CO 2−. highly [energetic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/energetics "Learn more about energetic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Indeed, the single-electron reduction of CO 2 has a strongly negative electrochemical potential (−1.90 V vs [Normal Hydrogen Electrode](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/normal-hydrogen-electrode "Learn more about Normal Hydrogen Electrode from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") — NHE) [[22]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b22). Other proposed reaction mechanisms bypass the formation of the radical CO 2−. by involving a series of multiple proton-coupled [electron transfers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/electron-transport "Learn more about electron transfers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (PCET) thus requiring less electrochemical potential [[23]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b23), [[24]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b24). CO 2 adsorption on a [semiconductor surface](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/semiconductor-surface "Learn more about semiconductor surface from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is one method of activating the otherwise inactive molecule for reduction [[22]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b22). In general, the surface atoms of the [photocatalyst](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/photocatalysts "Learn more about photocatalyst from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") coordinate with the carbon or the oxygen atoms of the CO 2 molecule as shown in [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#fig1). The different binding modes partly determine the reaction pathways [[21]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b21). Depending on the reaction pathway and the number of electron transferred, it is possible to obtain multiple different products such as [carbon monoxide](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/carbon-monoxide "Learn more about carbon monoxide from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [formic acid](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/formic-acid "Learn more about formic acid from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), formaldehyde, methanol and methane [[25]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b25). Among many products obtainable from CO 2, CO is a gaseous compound that requires the transfer of only two electrons, and thus it is a kinetically favorable choice as compared for example to methane, which requires the transfer of eight electrons to form one molecule as shown in [Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#tbl1). Moreover, a mixture of CO and H 2, coming from the proton reduction of water, can be employed in chemical reactors to produce more complex hydrocarbons through the Fischer–Tropsch process [[26]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b26), [[27]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b27). The obtained energy rich fuels such as methanol or methane, can be already used in the current infrastructure [[28]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/

*   Published: 25 June 2025

Advancing photoelectrochemical systems for sustainable energy and chemical production: challenges and opportunities
===================================================================================================================

*   [Zhaoyi Xi](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#auth-Zhaoyi-Xi-Aff1-Aff2)[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#Aff1),[2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#Aff2)&
*   [Mingzhao Liu](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#auth-Mingzhao-Liu-Aff1)[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#Aff1)

[_npj Materials Sustainability_](https://www.nature.com/npjmatsustain)**volume 3**, Article number:18 (2025) [Cite this article](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#citeas)

Abstract
--------

Photoelectrochemical (PEC) systems offer a promising approach to harness solar energy for producing essential chemicals and sustainable fuels. This perspective highlights their potential for generating hydrogen, oxygen, chlorine, ammonia, hydrogen peroxide, and carbon-based fuels. It also discusses key challenges, such as efficiency, selectivity, and scalability, as well as recent advances in catalyst design and system optimization that enhance practicality and promote real-world adoption.

Introduction
------------

The conversion of solar energy through photocatalytic generation of clean fuels via artificial photosynthesis offers both economic and environmental benefits to society. This technology not only supports a more sustainable energy structure but also reduces greenhouse gas emissions, contributing to the mitigation of global warming. Therefore, it holds the potential to significantly contribute to achieving carbon neutrality across the U.S. by 2050[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#ref-CR1 "Fujishima, A. & Honda, K. Electrochemical photolysis of water at a semiconductor electrode. Nature 238, 37–38 (1972)."),[2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#ref-CR2 "Nozik, A. J. Photoelectrochemistry: applications to solar energy conversion. Annu. Rev. Phys. Chem. 29, 189–222 (1978)."),[3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#ref-CR3 "Walter, M. G. et al. Solar water splitting cells. Chem. Rev. 110, 6446–6473 (2010)."). With the growing dependence on fossil fuels, the urgency for alternative and renewable energy sources has never been greater. In general, all artificial photosynthesis processes resemble the photosynthesis in green plants (Fig. [1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#Fig1))[4](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#ref-CR4 "Low, J., Yu, J., Jaroniec, M., Wageh, S. & Al-Ghamdi, A. A. Heterojunction photocatalysts. Adv. Mater. 29, 1601694 (2017)."),[5](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#ref-CR5 "Hou, H., Zeng, X. & Zhang, X. Production of hydrogen peroxide by photocatalytic processes. Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 59, 17356–17376 (2020)."). In the first step, excitons or electron-hole pairs are created through photon absorption by a molecular or semiconductor chromophore. Subsequently, the excitons separate into free electrons and holes that migrate to reaction centers, which can be enzymes or surface co-catalysts. Finally, light-independent redox reactions will proceed, forming reduced and oxidized products at spatially separated centers on the surface of the photocatalyst, where the separated carriers interact with chemical species on the surface, facilitating photocatalytic reactions. The overall energy conversion efficiency is determined by the efficiency of light absorption, the fraction of exciton separation before recombination, and the rate of light-independent redox reactions.

**Fig. 1**

[![Image 3: figure 1](https://media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs44296-025-00061-z/MediaObjects/44296_2025_61_Fig1_HTML.png)](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z/figures/1)

A simplified diagram for photosynthesis process in green plants.

[Full size image](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z/figures/1)

Among various artificial photosynthesis strategies, photoelectrochemical (PEC) systems convert free energy of sunlight into electrical energy, immediately before storing it in the form of chemical energy through electrochemical reactions[6](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#ref-CR6 "Garche, J. Encyclopedia of Electrochemical Power Sources 2nd edn, 409–422 (Elsevier, 2025)."). In a typical PEC cell, solar light is harvested by a semiconductor photoelectrode, the generation of charge carriers, which participate in oxidation and reduction reactions at separated electrodes, will result in the production of hydrogen and oxygen[7](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#ref-CR7 "Clarizia, L., Nadagouda, M. N. & Dionysiou, D. D. Recent advances and challenges of photoelectrochemical cells for hydrogen production. Curr. Opin. Green. Sustain. Chem. 41, 100825 (2023)."). PEC systems have emerged as one of the most promising solutions for artificial photosynthesis, directly harnessing solar energy to drive interfacial electrochemical (EC) reactions that produce essential fuels and chemical stocks. These systems enable PEC reactions on photoelectrodes, facilitating artificial photosynthesis to generate desired chemical products. The PEC systems have been widely explored as an approach for solar-drive water splitting and are often considered as an effective replacement for conventional electrochemical (EC) and photovoltaic-electrolysis (PV-EC) systems. According to various research in the past decades[8](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#ref-CR8 "Yang, W., Prabhakar, R. R., Tan, J., Tilley, S. D. & Moon, J. Strategies for enhancing the photocurrent, photovoltage, and stability of photoelectrodes for photoelectrochemical water splitting. Chem. Soc. Rev. 48, 4979–5015 (2019)."),[9](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#ref-CR9 "Jia, J. et al. Solar water splitting by photovoltaic-electrolysis with a solar-to-hydrogen efficiency over 30%. Nat. Commun. 7, 13237 (2016)."),[10](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#ref-CR10 "Parkinson, B. On the efficiency and stability of photoelectrochemical devices. Acc. Chem. Res. 17, 431–437 (1984)."),[11](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#ref-CR11 "Khaselev, O., Bansal, A. & Turner, J. A. High-efficiency integrated multijunction photovoltaic/electrolysis systems for hydrogen production. Int. J. Hydrog. Energy 26, 127–132 (2001)."), depending on the choice of the photoelectrodes and device structure, the solar-to-hydrogen (STH) efficiencies of PEC systems typically vary within 2%–10%. However, it remains lower than PV-EC systems (~10% STH) and EC systems which generate EFE over 15% with renewable electricity. In terms of stability, PEC systems can operate continuously for over 1000 h under optimized conditions, which is comparable towards PV-EC and EC systems. However, due to the electrode degradation and system integration problems, PEC systems still face scalability and economic-friendly challenges. Since the oxidation and reduction reaction respectively happen on the anode and the cathode, the oxidation and reduction products can be readily separated without cross-contamination. PEC systems also offer various advantages, such as reduced energy loss, inherent production separation, cost-effectiveness, and direct solar-to-chemical energy conversion[12](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#ref-CR12 "Ahmed, M. & Dincer, I. A review on photoelectrochemical hydrogen production systems: challenges and future directions. Int. J. Hydrog. Energy 44, 2474–2507 (2019)."). In a PEC cell, the light absorption first happens on a semiconductor photoelectrode. When photons with energy greater than the band gap energy (_E_ g) are absorbed, electron-hole pairs are generated as electrons are excited to the conduction band (CB), leaving corresponding holes in the valence band (VB). The second part of the reaction involves the separation of photogenerated carriers and their transportation toward electrolyte interface and back-contact. In the final stage, the interfacial charge transfers complete the redox processes (Fig. [2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#Fig2)). Most typically, the photoelectrode is an n-type semiconductor that serves as a photoanode that produces oxidized products, while the electrons travel to the (dark) cathode to produce reduced product. Alternatively, p-type semiconductors may serve as photocathodes and send photoholes to the (dark) anode. Since the photovoltage generated by a single photoelectrode is usually insufficient to drive the full cell reaction, an external DC bias is often needed. For this reason, there have been attempts to combine photocathode and photoanode in a common cell, which helps to improve photovoltage and photon utilization. PEC systems hold the promise of producing a variety of critically important chemical fuels and feedstocks, such as hydrogen (H 2), chlorine (Cl 2), ammonia (NH 3), hydrogen peroxide (H 2 O 2), and carbon-based fuels such as hydrocarbons and alcohols.

**Fig. 2: Schematic diagram of PEC water splitting cell based on an n-type semiconductor photoanode.**

[![Image 4: figure 2](https://media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs44296-025-00061-z/MediaObjects/44296_2025_61_Fig2_HTML.png)](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z/figures/2)

At the contact, an n-type Schottky barrier is formed for charge carrier separation. In the diagram, _L_ _λ_ stands for the mean photon penetration depth and _L_ _h_ stands for width of the Schottky barrier, in which the charge carrier separation is most efficient due to the presence of an electric field.

[Full size image](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z/figures/2)

Although PEC systems have been extensively studied for the generation of fuels and chemical compounds, there are still several aspects which limit the broad application of PEC techniques to large-scale industrial applications. In this perspective, we will give an overview on the status of studying PEC systems for these chemical products. The main will focus on recent research progress toward improving the stability and catalytic activity development of photoelectrode materials for PEC generation of several key chemicals, including hydrogen, chlorine, ammonia, hydrogen peroxide, hydrocarbons, and alcohols.

PEC water splitting for hydrogen production
-------------------------------------------

Hydrogen is a clean chemical fuel with a very high energy density of 120 MJ/kg. Although the absence of a large-scale hydrogen delivery infrastructure has limited its direct use as a chemical fuel, hydrogen has been an important chemical feedstock for many industrial chemical processes, such as oil refining, ammonia synthesis, and methanol production. In 2023, about 97 million tonnes of hydrogen were produced globally, out of which less than 1% was produced with low carbon emission. The dominant process for hydrogen production remains the steam reforming of fossil fuels and coal gasification, both associated with significant carbon footprint.

PEC water splitting that generates hydrogen (H 2) and oxygen (O 2) is the earliest studied and best-known system for artificial photosynthesis (Fig. [2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#Fig2)), attracting active materials science research over the past half century[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#ref-CR1 "Fujishima, A. & Honda, K. Electrochemical photolysis of water at a semiconductor electrode. Nature 238, 37–38 (1972)."),[2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#ref-CR2 "Nozik, A. J. Photoelectrochemistry: applications to solar energy conversion. Annu. Rev. Phys. Chem. 29, 189–222 (1978)."),[3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#ref-CR3 "Walter, M. G. et al. Solar water splitting cells. Chem. Rev. 110, 6446–6473 (2010)."),[13](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#ref-CR13 "Li, S. et al. Defect engineering of ternary Cu–In–Se quantum dots for boosting photoelectrochemical hydrogen generation. Carbon Energy 5, e384 (2023)."),[14](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#ref-CR14 "Guo, H.-S. et al. Photoelectrochemical water splitting improved by a cucurbit[7]uril-induced ternary heterojunction. Chem. Eng. J. 505, 159340 (2025)."). The primary advantage of this reaction arises from the fact that its only chemical feedstock, water, is one of the most earth-abundant resources in the world. Although water splitting only requires a cell voltage of 1.23 V thermodynamically, the kinetic barriers associated with the hydrogen evolving reaction (HER) and oxygen evolving reaction (OER) generally contribute to significant overpotential. It is the OER, which requires 4 electrons transferred for each O 2 molecule produced, that limits the overall reaction rate. Vast majority of PEC water splitting research focus on developing OER photoanode materials to overcome this barrier. This is also why early studies of PEC water splitting had been focused on wide band gap oxides that have deep enough valence band edge for OER.

As a wide band gap, chemically robust semiconductor, titanium dioxide (TiO 2,_E_ _g_ = 3.2 eV) is the classical example of photoanode materials for water splitting[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#ref-CR1 "Fujishima, A. & Honda, K. Electrochemical photolysis of water at a semiconductor electrode. Nature 238, 37–38 (1972)."). Despite being a promising candidate, it still suffers from charge recombination and limited light absorption issues. Yu et al.[15](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#ref-CR15 "Yu, Z., Liu, H., Zhu, M., Li, Y. & Li, W. Interfacial charge transport in 1D TiO2 based photoelectrodes for photoelectrochemical water splitting. Small 17, 1903378 (2021).") utilized 1D TiO 2 based semiconductor as photocatalyst for solar water splitting and summarized that different shapes of TiO 2, such as nanotubes, nanorods, and nanowires can influence the photocatalysts’ activity. Zhang et al.[16](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#ref-CR16 "Zhang, Z., Hossain, M. F. & Takahashi, T. Photoelectrochemical water splitting on highly smooth and ordered TiO2 nanotube arrays for hydrogen generation. Int. J. Hydrog. Energy 35, 8528–8535 (2010).") synthesized highly smooth and ordered TiO 2 nanotube for PEC hydrogen generation with a two-step anodization method, which comes up with a photoconversion efficiency of 0.49% compared to one-step of 0.18%, under AM 1.5 illumination. Wang et al.[17](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#ref-CR17 "Wang, W. et al. Heterostructured TiO2 nanorod@Nanobowl arrays for efficient photoelectrochemical water splitting. Small 12, 1469–1478 (2016).") utilized nanosphere lithography and hydrothermal method to grow TiO 2 nanorods (NR), which achieved a double PEC water splitting efficiency of 1.24 mA cm−2, compared to the conventional TiO 2 NR arrays. Improved light scattering, charge separation and the formation of heterojunction cause the improvement for performance. Li group synthesized TiO 2 nanowire (NW) arrays[18](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#ref-CR18 "Wang, G. et al. Hydrogen-treated TiO2 nanowire arrays for photoelectrochemical water splitting. Nano Lett. 11, 3026–3033 (2011).") with hydrogen-treated. Due to the higher donor density originates from oxygen vacancies, the PEC water splitting performance is significantly enhanced. This strategy also applied to rutile nanowires and anatase nanotubes, further offers potential improvements in PEC water splitting, dye-sensitized solar cells, and photocatalysis.

Zinc oxide (ZnO, _E_ _g_ = 3.3 eV), as another wide band gap semiconductor with excellent charge carrier mobility, has also been extensively studied[19](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#ref-CR19 "Liu, M., Nam, C.-Y., Black, C. T., Kamcev, J. & Zhang, L. Enhancing water splitting activity and chemical stability of zinc oxide nanowire photoanodes with ultrathin titania shells. J. Phys. Chem. C. 117, 13396–13402 (2013)."). A variety of microscopic morphologies of ZnO electrodes were investigated, including nanorod, nanowire, and thin films. Deshmukh et al.[20](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#ref-CR20 "Deshmukh, P. R., Sohn, Y. & Shin, W. G. Chemical synthesis of ZnO nanorods: investigations of electrochemical performance and photo-electrochemical water splitting applications. J. Alloy. Compd. 711, 573–580 (2017).") employed ZnO nanorods (NR) by a chemical route, which is facile and inexpensive under low temperature. As a result, the ZnO NR exhibits 97% stability over 3000 CV cycles, which is competitive among the photocatalysts. Chen et al.[21](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#ref-CR21 "Chen, H. M. et al. Quantum dot monolayer sensitized ZnO nanowire-array photoelectrodes: true efficiency for water splitting. Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 49, 5966–5969 (2010).") synthesized nanolayer ZnO nanowire (NW) which is quantum dot. The ZnO NW promotes the photoconversion efficiency to more than 200% compared to pristine ZnO NW, with a relatively reliable stability in aqueous solution.

Tungsten oxide (WO 3, _E_ _g_ = 2.8 eV) is also widely studied as a water splitting photocatalyst and is notable for its high stability in acidic electrolytes. Cristino et al.[22](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#ref-CR22 "Cristino, V. et al. Efficient photoelectrochemical water splitting by anodically grown WO3 electrodes. Langmuir 27, 7276–7284 (2011).") synthesized WO 3 photoanodes by tungsten anodization, which showed promising PEC water oxidation performance in 1 M H 2 SO 4. The promotion of the performance originated from the higher surface activity, as well as the better charge transfer rate. According to a study by Liu et al., electron-hole recombination is reduced in Gd-doped WO 3 synthesized via hydrothermal method[23](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#ref-CR23 "Liu, Y. et al. Enhancement of the photoelectrochemical performance of WO3 vertical arrays film for solar water splitting by gadolinium doping. J. Phys. Chem. C 119, 14834–14842 (2015)."). The Gd-doped thin film also exhibited higher photocurrent and increased IPCE, therefore it showed increased PEC water splitting efficiency. Kalanur et al.[24](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#ref-CR24 "Kalanur, S. S., Yoo, I.-H., Eom, K. & Seo, H. Enhancement of photoelectrochemical water splitting response of WO3 by means of Bi doping. J. Catal. 357, 127–137 (2018).") investigated the Bi-doped WO 3 thin films. The Bi-doped thin film achieves a solar energy conversion efficiency of 0.4% at 1.13 V vs. RHE. The enhanced PEC activity is due to the band gap modulation, as well as the higher charge carrier density. Zhang et al. reported the fabrication of WO 3/Sb 2 S 3 heterojunctions with different morphologies[25](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#ref-CR25 "Zhang, J., Liu, Z. & Liu, Z. Novel WO3/Sb2S3 heterojunction photocatalyst based on WO3 of different morphologies for enhanced efficiency in photoelectrochemical water splitting. ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 8, 9684–9691 (2016)."). The WO 3/Sb 2 S 3 heterojunction photoelectrodes show improved PEC water splitting performance with a photocurrent density of 1.79 mA cm−2. Because of the improved charge separation and light absorption property, as well as the appropriate energy gaps and special WO 3 structure.

Although wide band gap oxides provide large enough photovoltage for OER, they cannot support high efficiency solar fuel generation, as only 6% of solar radiation is in the ultraviolet band. Photoelectrode materials with band gap in the visible light spectrum is much more desirable[26](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#ref-CR26 "Joy, J., Mathew, J. & George, S. C. Nanomaterials for photoelectrochemical water splitting—review. Int. J. Hydrog. Energy 43, 4804–4817 (2018)."),[27](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#ref-CR27 "Zhou, C. et al. Pulsed laser deposition of delafossite oxide thin films on YSZ (001) substrates as solar water splitting photocathodes. Environ. Sci. Nano 12, 241–247 (2025)."),[28](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#ref-CR28 "Hilbrands, A. M. et al. Impact of varying the photoanode/catalyst interfacial composition on solar water oxidation: the case of BiVO4(010)/FeOOH photoanodes. J. Am. Chem. Soc. 145, 23639–23650 (2023)."). For solar water splitting, bismuth vanadate (BiVO 4, BVO) has been recognized as one of the most promising visible light photoanode materials, with many advantages such as relatively narrow bandgap (2.4–2.6 eV), proper band alignment with water redox reactions, and superior chemical stability[29](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#ref-CR29 "Zachos, J. C., Dickens, G. R. & Zeebe, R. E. An early Cenozoic perspective on greenhouse warming and carbon-cycle dynamics. Nature 451, 279–283 (2008)."),[30](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#ref-CR30 "Wu, H.-L., Li, X.-B., Tung, C.-H. & Wu, L.-Z. Semiconductor quantum dots: an emerging candidate for CO2 photoreduction. Adv. Mater. 31, 1900709 (2019)."). Zhou et al. studied lithium as a unique dopant in BVO that boosts the water oxidation performance, both experimentally and computationally[31](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#ref-CR31 "Zhou, C. et al. Interstitial lithium doping in BiVO4 thin film photoanode for enhanced solar water splitting activity. Chem. Mater. 32, 6401–6409 (2020)."). With the optimized 5 at.% Li-BVO, the water oxidation activity increase by about 20%. The promotion effect owes to Li acts as a shallow electron donor in BVO, which improves the majority carrier concentration. Besides doping elements into BVO to improve, the fabrication conditions are also investigated. Xi et al.[32](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#ref-CR32 "Xi, Z., Zhou, C., Kisslinger, K. & Liu, M. Optimizing pulsed laser deposition of crystalline BiVO4 thin films on yttrium-stabilized zirconia (110) substrates. Chem. Commun. 60, 9974–9977 (2024).") investigated the optimization of BVO deposition conditions over YSZ (110) substrate. As a result, an optimized two-step-deposition (TSD) method can independently tune nucleation and bulk film growth, which results in the formation of phase pure crystalline BVO. Other deposition conditions, such as oxygen pressure and deposition temperatures are also extensively studied.

Despite those progresses, the most significant problem of PEC water splitting is that it must produce oxygen as a byproduct of hydrogen generation. However, as the second most abundant species in Earth atmosphere, oxygen has negligible economic value. For this reason, it would be a fruitful approach to apply the vast knowledge accumulated from PEC water splitting studies to other PEC processes that produce chemicals that have more significant economic impact.

PEC chlorine generation
-----------------------

Chlorine serves as a key chemical product and important feedstock, with broad applications ranging from public sanitation to the production of PVC and other chemicals. By 2023, the annual global production of chlorine has exceeded 100 million tonnes. The industrial generation of chlorine almost exclusively relies on the chloralkali process, which accounts for approximately 1% of the global electricity consumption[33](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#ref-CR33 "Fauvarque, J. The chlorine industry. Pure Appl. Chem. 68, 1713–1720 (1996)."). Because of the virtually unlimited supply of chloride ions throughout the oceans, PEC chlorine generation becomes a very attractive alternative to PEC water splitting[34](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#ref-CR34 "Junge, C. E. & Werby, R. T. The concentration of chloride, sodium, potassium, calcium, and sulfate in rain water over the united states. J. Atmos. Sci. 15, 417–425 (1958)."),[35](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#ref-CR35 "Man, J. H. K. et al. Photoelectrochemical activation of free chlorine utilizing vacancies-engineered BiVO4 photoanode for simultaneous pharmaceuticals and personal care products (PPCPs) degradation and bacterial inactivation: An integrative batch and continuous flow reactor study. Sep. Purif. Technol. 360, 130959 (2025)."). In this vision, PEC water splitting would be conducted in seawater or brine, with H 2 produced at the cathode in the same way, but with Cl 2 rather than O 2 produced at the anode, offering a promising, cost-effective approach to solar hydrogen production with the added economic value of chlorine (Fig. [3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44296-025-00061-z#Fig3)). The chlorine generation reaction (\(2{{\rm{Cl}}}^{-}\to {{\rm{Cl}}}_{2}+2{e}^{-}\), ClER) is about 160 mV more anodic than OER[36](https

enriched_points.expanded_content: Photoelectrochemical (PEC) processes utilize semiconductor materials to convert solar energy into chemical fuels, specifically hydrogen via water splitting, enhancing renewable energy utilization.
Photocatalytic methods involve light-activated catalysts that promote chemical reactions, such as CO2 reduction, transforming solar energy directly into storable fuels.
Thermochemical approaches leverage high temperatures to drive endothermic reactions, using concentrated solar energy for fuel synthesis, especially hydrogen and hydrocarbons.
Comparing PEC, photocatalytic, and thermochemical methods reveals variations in efficiency, scalability, and product types, guiding the selection of optimal solar fuel production strategies.
Emerging technologies like hybrid systems and advanced materials are enhancing solar fuel production, improving efficiencies, and enabling broader applications in sustainable energy solutions.
